speaker: interviewer
text: Toronto. I'm Paula Draper, and I'm interviewing Dennis Urstein. And this is tape five. OK, we were talking about the time you were in the-- the roofing commando in Auschw-- in Birkenau now. Is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm. That's right. Well, you know, like I said, that gave us-- ugh, gave us-- we had access because-- because of the nature of the beast. You went everywhere, you know? Every camp, you know, there were sections, A, B, C, D, E. And that was a women camp, so we-- wherever there was a job required, that's where we went.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were women treated differently than men in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Well, that's something that's inbred. You know, when-- where I come from-- if I may use that term-- women were put on a pedestal. Uh, you didn't speak badly to women. Maybe some people did, but not where I-- where I was, OK? Women were something special. Uh, women didn't work, although they worked their-- their-- their heads off at home, but I say the term of worked in an office, or something like that, although some did. And when I saw how women were treated by women, mind you not, SS wome-- SS men. SS women and kapo women. You know-- OK, I'll give you a really-- a quickie down there that-- that-- that signifies what I'm saying. I forgot about that-- that-- I had a job very briefly for about four or five days that, uh-- that, uh, picked up kettles of soup. That was in the very beginning, [INAUDIBLE], after the [INAUDIBLE], after the tailor shop, whatever, in between somewhere. I don't know. I can't get it straight. Anyway, and we delivered soup to a-- a-- kommandos, in other words, the work details that worked outside the camp. And, uh, one day we, uh-- we-- it was in a truck, a motorized truck, a camion. And we drove up with these kettles to deliver the soup at lunch time. And, uh, we burned ourselves because sometimes they weren't completely closed, and-- and the roads down there were bumpy. You know, they were these lousy paved roads or something. And we brought the soup down. And then I saw a [? frau ?] kommando, a women work details. And she wanted me-- [SIGHS]. You know it's-- [SIGHS]. I saw these-- these-- these-- these-- these-- these girls, they were all girls, with, their sack-like clothe with their head shorn, filthy, dirty. And then I saw Inge, I forgot to tell you about that. Inge was a girl that went to school with me. And I-- I recognized her.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she looked at me. I was just putting the kettle down, the soup kettle. And her eyes were dead. The one thing that you learn in camp, you always knew if somebody is going to die. Not in an hour, but tomorrow, or tonight, or the next day. And she looked awful. And you know, to me, she's still the most precious girl that I've ever known in my life. It's an impression that I can't shake. And I-- you know what? I see her right now. I don't s-- I don't conjure a lot of pictures in my head. I never do. But her, I can see right now. We saw women march and being beaten. We were beaten, but it didn't hurt me. But it hurt them. They had same facilities, or non-facilities, that we had. You couldn't wash properly. You had no soap. But you were supposed to be clean. So how do they-- how you going to be clean if you haven't got any soap? How are you going to be clean if you haven't got access to where there was enough water to wash yourself? Or to be bumped around because you're washing and somebody else that is stronger wants to wash first.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the way they walked-- now we did-- I'm sure we didn't walk any different. But you see, I could see them. I couldn't see myself. Let's go back to-- I want to quit this because this-- this hurts. You know, there's certain things that I always thought I'd get over with. I don't. It-- it hurts.

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked with, uh-- with the roofers. And, uh, this is now 1900 and 40-- 43? Yeah, at the end of '43, I think. No. See? Uh, dates, this is crazy. Anyhow, we were-- we were coming back, and we were on roll call-- this is an incident I did not tell you. And, uh, uh, we were standing there to be counted. And all of a sudden a guy on a motorcycle, a-- a-- a chauffeur, drove up. And he went over to the, uh-- to the, uh block elders, and he called the scribe-- you know the guy that-- that-- we used to call him schreiber, OK? And, uh, he came and he picked out 50 guys. In our barracks, there were about 200 or 220 or something like that. 50, and we were put aside and I said, oh my god, what selections? We don't look that bad to be selected, you know? [SIGHS]. A truck came up and that was the truck that used to carry the bodies to the crematorium. And we were all asked-- not shouted at or not beaten-- to get on the truck. Just get on the truck, you know? Get on the truck. And, uh, we drive. And all of a sudden, we are driving into a-- a path where there is grass on the left and on the right. And we see the crematori-- that's the first time I saw the crematorium. I saw it a second time at number five at a later date. What number was this one?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I can't remember. I do not remember. I don't. I'm not sorry. It's down there. We are asked to get off, very politely. And this was, uh-- the roll call was in the-- in the late evening, like 5:00, 6:00. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We're going downstairs. There are steps that are going downstairs. We're standing in front of a door that has, uh, a iron bar, short iron bar, locked. There's a window, small little window. It wasn't actually a window. It was like a-- like one of those, uh, holes that you have in the door in order to look out or in or something like that. Two SS men come. They put on gas masks, open the door, and you saw hell. Hell on earth. A whole room full of dead bodies. We found out later-- two days later-- that there was a revolt, or something like that, of the Sonderkommando of Greeks. And they had, uh, two chambers full of dead bodies that they gassed before, and nobody could take them out. So they took us. Because you see, the-- the idea, if-- if ever you stepped across the line into a crematorium, that the only way you could do that-- or two ways you could do it-- either you were dead, or to be shot, or you work in the Sonderkommando. But nobody from the outside was-- was-- was-- this was taboo. This was a taboo in there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, we are given, uh, uh, short straps of leather straps. And some of the-- the guys got the iron hooks down there that are bent with a point down there. And we're told to take the Jew bastards out. And we had this odor, and you start to cough. They had gas masks on. We start to cough. It was like, uh, uh, [FRENCH], something like that, or-- or mustard or-- a horrible smell. And-- and that sight, don't even ask. You see bodies that were-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] some people had their-- their-- their fingers in somebody else's eye socket. You saw, uh, usually, uh, children on the very bottom, and you have people on top of them. And then you see stronger people on the top, as-- as if if they would gasp for air down there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had to drag them out, which we did. Then we got hoses. We had to wash them off, and we had to wash off the room. It took us almost until about, oh, 3:00, 4:00 in the morning. And then there were some trolleys down there where we had to put them. And then these trolleys went up to the ovens. And then we didn't-- we didn't cremate anything. And we were wet, and dirty, and filthy, and coughing like hell. I should have gotten lung cancer down there. And we went back to the barracks. I'm an amateur photographer, that's my hobby. If I ever wanted a camera, I wanted to have a camera, not to take to the dead people in the crematorium. I wanted to have a camera to put on the people that saw us when we was-- when we w-- w-- went to our barracks. They thought we were-- we were ghosts because if-- when they took us, they knew that they didn't take us to, uh-- to a dance or something like that. They knew something-- they-- they thought that we would be executed. They didn't say or knew that we were going to go to the crematorium. They just thought by some reason or others.

speaker: interviewee
text: But you know, the funny pa-- the-- the-- the-- the funny part? It's not funny. The thing that occurred to me within two or three hours, I said to myself, I don't think you're going to get killed. And I answered myself, you know why? Because the scribe didn't take my number. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And if he didn't take your number, you weren't written off. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Did anyone say anything while you were working?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not a word. Not one word.

speaker: interviewer
text: About--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think that anybody, nobody. I don't remember anybody saying boo. The only voices that we heard was-- then later on, there were three SS men down there, were, schneller, schneller, quicker, quicker, quicker. You know, work faster, da da da, and that's all we heard.

speaker: interviewer
text: About--

speaker: interviewee
text: But nobody beat us, funny enough.

speaker: interviewer
text: About how many bodies were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd say about eight, 900 hundred. That's my guess.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you estimate the size of the gas chamber?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Oh, we saw it. We were-- there we really had a look of what the gas chamber-- you know, we were talking, being gassed. We didn't know what gas-- while we were in the camp, who the hell knew what a gas chamber looked like? We didn't know. We were gassed. Wir wurden vergast. That's it. But we didn't know what it looked like.

speaker: interviewee
text: It looked like, uh, in the ceiling, it was-- the ceiling was about, oh, I guess as high as that ceiling here, which is, what? About two and a half meters, three meters, something like that? And there were some, uh-- some, uh, shower heads that were built in down there. And there was a column in the center, a column that had wire around it. And that's, when be washed it up we found, uh, crystals down there that were-- that were still there. We didn't know this-- this was Zyklon B. You know, nobody's a smart ass. We-- we-- we do [INAUDIBLE]. You know that afterwards, but you didn't know it at the time. Ah, I knew, that what-- what-- not what type, but that gas was involved because while I worked in Kanada, there was a hut down there in Kanada where gas was stored. And this SS man that I-- I-- I-- we found in 1900 and what was it? 1900 and 70-- I think I gave you the date on that thing, K, didn't I? In 1972, he was brought on trial in Vienna that worked in Kanada. He was the [GERMAN], in other words, the meter reader.

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- he received a message to take his truck that had, uh, a red cross on-- on-- on-- on two sides and one on the top, which was one of the small delivery truck-like things. And he went to the hut. He had the key. He took out the cans. We knew it was gas, but we didn't know what it was for. And he put it in the truck. And he went into-- to the crematoriums down there and did his job. That was Franz Wunsch.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when did you hear this? This was after and during the trial?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. We knew that-- oh, we knew that while we were working down there, that he did that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you ever heard the expression of a shower or anything like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not until I saw it myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you tell people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yep. Yes, I did. As many as I could reach, which weren't too many because you didn't trust anybody, for something like that for-- for one reason only. Remember I told you this story about when you burn yourself once, you'll be twice as shy? When-- when this came out of me with this school pal of mine, where-- where-- when he asked me where his father was and his sister, and I told him there. I learned a lesson, so I told the two guys from Vienna that we are working with me in Kanada about that. And they said, we knew about it. They knew about it. They knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this the only time you witnessed this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh no, no, no. There was another time. Uh, the other time was, uh, during the, uh, um, uh, when the, uh, Hungarian Jews came in, and I was still with the roofers. And, uh, one day-- we never knew where we were going to work. You know, kapo went in the front, several went in the front down there and reported so many people going out at the door. There was a hut down there, you know, with a guard and-- and a guy that was the main, uh, registrar and wrote in that kommando so and so-- for instance, us, dachdecker kommando, [GERMAN], walked-- 200 men walked out. And we walked. And all of a sudden, we walked, and we walked, and we walked, and we walked to-- to crematorium. It was another crematorium. I think-- I believe it was number five. It was the last one on the right hand side. And the man in charge down there-- all-- not only there, but in charge of all five crematoriums were Hauptscharfuhrer Otto Moll, M-O-L-L, the cyclops. He had one eye, one glass eye. And behind his [? bag, ?] he was it. I mean, this is mister-- if you talk about Auschwitz, you talk about Otto Moll. He was it. He was the guy that, uh-- [SIGHS] there, I saw some Sonderkommando, never at work but I saw-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] apparently what happened was that, uh, the capacity for, uh, gas people was enough. But the cremation facilities weren't large enough, and they worked day and night. And the reason that we went down there, uh, were to fix the roof because the shingles took off around the-- the chimney. And we walked in there. Otto Moll gave us a reception and told us in no uncertain terms, first of all everybody's a swine. That's the address that we received, [? wir ?] schweine. Anybody's looking around himself, I'm a good shot. And then he just pointed his pistol and shot a guy down there-- from the sonderkommando-- right in his leg, or in his hip, or something, just to show us what kind of a hero he was with-- with how-- how well he can handle a gun. And we are put on the roof. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Uh, curiosity kills the cat, and as a human being, every human, you're curious, no matter how dangerous it may be, or how rough the surroundings, or how somebody threatens you. I don't know. It's like somebody say close your eyes, and you just go like through and he think your eyes are closed, but then you see something. They talk about-- there were-- it must have been about five, four or five-- five, five ditches, long ditches, about, I'd say, 40 meters long, eight meters wide. Big bodies that were just smoldering down there. And new bodies came in. I put on top of that. They even created a drain to drain the fat off on the bottom at an angle. That's how the ditch was constructed. And Mr. Moll was running around like a dompteur in a circus, looking at lions. And, uh, that was very bad. That was really bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you say the worst moment was for you in all of this? [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at that time, only later. It was when my parents came. That was the worst time. See, I think that I'm, again, if you can call it lucky, that I didn't realize the enormity of what I saw. Although I knew what's going to happen, it just didn't-- it didn't hit me because I-- I strongly believed that if I would have taken all these things as they were, I wouldn't be sitting here. You know, a lot of people committed suicide in many ways, like, uh, going to the wire, electrocuted. The guys on the guard tower shot him on top of it, so you were dead anyhow. So that was is it. Or you wanted to commit suicide in a-- in a different form, you drank the water. You got typhoid and you got  killed. I mean, if you wanted to kill yourself, there were many, many ways how you could kill yourself, quickly, or not as quickly. I didn't say slower, but not as quickly. But, uh, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] you know, there was so much hatred in me. That, I had all through that time. That-- although speaking, and, if there was such I thing as dreaming-- I do not believe that I ever dreamt, but maybe I did-- speaking, we spoke and created the most fantastic dishes. As a matter of fact, I remember an incident and that happened in, I think, in 1943, when a man, a prisoner, a haftling, an inmate, got killed because he couldn't stop talking about the food that he is going to create. Somebody in the-- in the barracks killed him for it because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the worst thing-- not incident, but the worst thing-- was hunger. Nobody that doesn't know what real-- and I mean, when I say real hunger it means nothing-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] doesn't know how much that hurts.

speaker: interviewer
text: You just mentioned anger.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Yeah, I wanted to kill them. I conjured during the day in my mind how I'm go about to kill Otto-- Otto [? Graf ?] or-- or Franz Wunsch or then after this incident when we were on the roof in crematorium five, what I would do with Otto Moll. And somehow it soothed me. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I was very angry. When I-- I know I'm jumping far ahead, but when I came out, I had only one priority, a priority. I had other things, but my priority was to find them and kill them. I literally, I say this. And in some cases, I succeeded. And I'm not sorry for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You've often said to me that you didn't want to do an interview because you weren't angry enough to do the interview.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all, you change. And again, priorities. I-- I-- I'm not going to say you know what my-- my priorities are to talk to young people and tell them what it's all about. This interview is not my prior-- it still isn't my priority. I mean, I know that the cause and the reason why it's being done is very honorable, and I'm very happy and glad that it's being done. That's fine. But that's not-- my priority is this, to talk to people today that all of a sudden resettlement, as they used to call us, to be resettled, today  is called ethnic cleansing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what's this whole horse shit all about? We're doing the same thing today that we-- that they did to us down there. Now we're doing it a different way. It's not as sophisticated anymore. OK, so you don't have people that come in, they have undressed. They get their hair shorn. They go into a gas chamber. If not, they go into the camp and being tortured, and killed, and murdered, and who knows what. And today, they're doing in a different way. So they walk into a village and-- and-- and the guy that lives next door all his life down there knocks the whole family off. What's the difference? And-- and nobody does anything about it. See, we, the survivors, always say one thing. We always say, hey, nobody cared about us. Nobody did anything for us. Are we doing anything for the Serbs or for the Croats or for the-- for the Muslims? We don't do anything about it either. So who the hell-- are we going to call the kettle black? [? Get ?] [? annoyed. ?] That makes me angry, very angry, and I can't vent it. Who can I vent it at? At my wife? At my beautiful daughter? At my sister? Or at you? Or at him? I can't do that. But I'm very angry. So that helps you speak to the kids?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. Because I want them to understand that it's all not, it's not-- I want them to understand that today is, whatever. Today's Tuesday, for instance, which it isn't. Today's Thurs-- Friday. But let us say today's Friday, and tomorrow morning, they wake up and everything they've done today is all gone. It's pfft. It's gone. It's not there anymore. They're pariahs. They don't exist. Anybody can do what he would. He can kick him. He can throw him out of the window. Nobody would care less.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the important part that they have to understand. Who do I going to go to? What they're going to do with this knowledge, I don't know. But at least I am satisfied that they know about it. They must know about it. Who's going to tell them? They're going to read a book by Mr. Green, Blue, or Yellow that he was number 4,262 and he was in Auschwitz, or in Belsen or something like that 20 years ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: Let Mr. Green, that wrote the book on his life, tell about this story today. Then he can do something. That's better than making money from his book. Forget about the dough for the book. That's not important. Maybe you can reach more people with the book, but if he walks into a class, or into a church, or into a synagogue, or into a theater-- I don't care-- on the street and he says, I have seen this happen. That is more important than a goddamn book, to me, anyhow. Maybe I'm wrong. I can only talk about myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned you were in the gypsy camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Some beautiful women down there. 1944. All of a sudden, like, you know, this is a-- a paradise comes about. We see whole families walking in in their, uh, beautiful, colorful dresses. Again, I'm not putting a label on that. It just happened. Gypsy dresses, beautiful. Young ones, older ones, men. We even-- you know, we even saw a couple of guys in there in a German uniform. They took them right from the front because he was a gypsy, and they put them into the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then overnight, it was quiet. All we saw-- because we were next door. We were in-- I think in D and they were in-- in E. You know, there were sections of the camp and it was only divided by-- by electrical wire. There were no guards at that point, you know? The guards were around the whole complex, but not in between the individual camps. And we saw the trucks and we knew-- we knew what happened. I knew. There were a couple of guys that I knew that were, as I call them, big men in the underground. And they told me that the gypsy camp is going to be evacuated. I said, what do you mean evacuated? He says, you know where. And the next day, almost whole night, the whole night, they did the same thing with the-- with the-- with the gyp-- with the Czech camp, with the-- the people from Theresienstadt. They just took the whole camp, and that was it. And-- and you know, you-- you-- you-- look, you have a choice-- you know, many people say to me, don't you think that you were like a bunch of goddamn sheeps? I have been asked that question by people in school. And I have-- it's not a trick. I think it's something that is effective. I tried to walk behind him. You know, I'm on one side, and then I walk behind him. And I take a pen-- a pen-- and I put them against their head and I said, OK, this is a gun. How are you going to fight me? I know it's very crude, but it works. That's what happened to us. OK, you had one choice. Weapons, you didn't have, I didn't have any. So how would you attack the guy that stands down there, that's well fed, that's strong. Maybe he's stupid, but he has all these facilities with him. What are you go-- how are you going to attack him? By scratching his eyes out? Or knocking him on the chin? So he kills you. So what have you achieved? Nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the tough part. You know, in-- after the horse has left the barn, we can all be smart asses. And-- and-- and-- and-- and-- and all the people that are-- that are writing books, and theories, about what is this, and what is that, and shoot this, and shoot that, hey, after the war, I know we shouldn't touch this, but very briefly, I want to--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, wait. We'll have to take a break right now though.

speaker: interviewee
text: Why? You have--
